content.problemStatement:
We have taken a great initiative to have a women only quota for admissions. The admission criteria is to solve one graph problem. Alice, being a woman, seeks admission under this quota and wants your help to solve the following problem.

You are given an integer NNN. Construct a good graph with NNN vertices.

A good graph is one that satisfies the following conditions:


The graph is simple (i.e, doesn't contain self-loops or multiedges), undirected, and **connected**.
If ∣i−j∣|i-j|∣i−j∣ is a prime number, then degi≠degjdeg_i \neq deg_jdegi​=degj​, where degideg_idegi​ is the degree of vertex iii.
Among all graphs on NNN vertices that satisfy the above two conditions, a good graph is one that then **minimizes the maximum degree**, i.e, max⁡i=1Ndegi\max_{i=1}^N deg_imaxi=1N​degi​ is minimized.
If such a graph exists, find any one of them. If no such graph exists, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the graph.


Output Format
For each test case, output the answer as follows:



If no good graph on NNN vertices exists, print −1-1−1 on a new line.
Otherwise, first print a single integer XXX: the maximum degree of a good graph on NNN vertices.
On the next line, print a single integer MMM: the number of edges in the graph you construct.
Then, print MMM lines. The iii-th of these lines should contain two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting an edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.

The graph you print should satisfy all the conditions laid out in the statement.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: N=2N = 2N=2, and we have deg1=deg2=1deg_1 = deg_2 = 1deg1​=deg2​=1 which is the best we can do.
Test case 222: N=3N = 3N=3, and we have deg1=deg2=1deg_1 = deg_2 = 1deg1​=deg2​=1 and deg3=2deg_3 = 2deg3​=2. ∣1−3∣=2|1-3| = 2∣1−3∣=2 is prime, and 111 and 333 have different degrees as required.
Test case 333: N=4N = 4N=4, and we have deg1=deg2=2deg_1 = deg_2 = 2deg1​=deg2​=2, deg3=deg4=1deg_3 = deg_4 = 1deg3​=deg4​=1. It's easy to verify that any pair with a difference of 222 or 333 have different degrees.
